---
title: "Generalised additive models: learning smooth shapes"
---

Sometimes a covariate's effect is not a straight line — think age, or a motion
nuisance. A **smooth term** $f(x)$ captures the shape without you specifying it.
The trick is to write the smooth as a linear combination of fixed wiggly building
blocks, $f(x) = B(x)\gamma$ — now it is **linear in $\gamma$** and drops straight
into the [GLM](glm.qmd) solve. To stop it overfitting, add a **roughness penalty**
$\lambda\,\gamma^{\top}S\gamma$, turning the solve into
$(X^{\top}WX + \lambda S)\beta = X^{\top}Wz$ — the GLM normal equations with one
extra term. The penalty strength $\lambda$ is chosen by
[Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd). And the payoff: **that penalty is a
random-effect variance in disguise**, so a GAM *is* an [LME](lme.qmd), and the two
chapters are one method. This is the rung where the suite's organising equivalence
stops being a promise and becomes a derivation.

Rung 3 of [the ladder](index.qmd). It builds on the [GLM](glm.qmd) (the penalised
solve) and pays off the [LME](lme.qmd) connection.

## From a coefficient to a shape

A GLM gives each covariate one coefficient — a straight-line effect. But many
effects are curved: a response that rises then falls with age, a nuisance whose
influence saturates. We want to estimate a **function** $f(x)$, not a slope, and
we don't want to commit in advance to "quadratic" or "cubic".

**A running example.** Take cortical thickness against age across the lifespan.
Thickness grows in childhood, plateaus, then declines — a clearly nonlinear
trajectory whose exact shape we do *not* know. We could guess a cubic, but a
cubic forces one global curvature and will fight the data wherever the true
curvature changes. We want a representation flexible enough to follow the real
curve, yet controllable enough not to chase measurement noise. The rest of the
page builds exactly that, and at scale: the same smooth basis is shared across
$V$ vertices while each vertex gets its own response (mass-univariate, the way
`gam_fit` is written).

## Make the shape linear again: basis expansion {#sec-basis}

Here is the move that unlocks everything else. We do not know $f$, but suppose
we fix in advance a small library of simple, known functions $B_1, \dots, B_K$ —
call them **basis functions** — and write the unknown $f$ as a *weighted sum* of
them:

$$
f(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} B_k(x)\,\gamma_k = B(x)\,\gamma .
$$ {#eq-basis}

The basis functions are fixed; only the weights $\gamma$ are unknown. The point
is that $f$ is now **linear in $\gamma$**, so "estimate a curve" has become
"estimate $K$ numbers" — and the entire GLM machinery applies unchanged: append
the row vector $B(x_i) = [B_1(x_i), \dots, B_K(x_i)]$ to observation $i$'s design
row, and the smooth's weights become additional coefficients. A rich basis (large
$K$) can trace almost any shape. The only open question is *which* basis — and
that choice is the whole craft of this chapter.

### The basis a reader would invent first — and why it fails

The most natural basis is the **monomials** $B_k(x) = x^{k-1}$, i.e. fit a
high-degree polynomial $f(x)=\gamma_0 + \gamma_1 x + \gamma_2 x^2 + \cdots$. It
is linear in $\gamma$, so it drops into the GLM, and with enough terms it can
bend any way we like. So why not stop here?

Two reasons, both fatal at scale:

- **Polynomials are global.** Each $B_k(x)=x^{k-1}$ is nonzero everywhere, so a
  data point near age 80 tugs on the coefficient that also controls the fit near
  age 5. A bump in one region ripples across the whole domain — the fit cannot be
  locally flexible without being globally wild.
- **High-degree polynomials oscillate.** Pushing the degree up to gain
  flexibility makes the fit swing violently between data points (the Runge
  phenomenon), and the design columns $1, x, x^2, \dots$ become nearly collinear,
  so $X^{\top}X$ is badly conditioned and the solve is numerically fragile.

The cure is to keep the "weighted sum of simple functions" idea but swap the
basis for one whose functions are **local**.

### B-splines: local bumps stitched at knots

Picture instead a row of overlapping, hump-shaped functions, each living over a
short stretch of the $x$-axis and zero outside it. A smooth curve is then a
weighted sum of these local humps: raise the weight on the hump sitting over age
40 and you lift the fit *there* and almost nowhere else. That locality is exactly
what polynomials lacked.

Those humps are **B-splines**. Concretely: lay down a grid of **knots** along the
$x$-axis. Between adjacent knots the B-spline is a low-degree polynomial piece
(cubic by default), and the pieces are stitched together so the overall function
is continuous up to its second derivative — smooth to the eye, with no visible
kinks. Each individual B-spline $B_k$ is nonzero over only a handful of
neighbouring knot-spans (a cubic B-spline touches four), so the design matrix
$B$ is **banded**: each row has only $\text{degree}+1$ nonzeros. A useful sanity
property is the **partition of unity** — at every $x$ the basis values sum to
one, $\sum_k B_k(x) = 1$ — so the basis can represent a constant exactly with
equal weights, and a row of $B$ is a local weighted average.

`nitrix` builds this in `basis.py`. `bspline_basis(x, n_basis, degree=3,
penalty_order=2)` constructs a **uniform cubic B-spline** design over the data
range. Under the hood `_bspline_design` maps each $x_i$ to a parametric
coordinate $s = (x_i - \text{lo})/(\text{hi}-\text{lo})\cdot(\,K-\text{degree})$,
finds which span it falls in (`floor(s)`), and fills the $\text{degree}+1$
nonzero entries of that row with the closed-form **uniform B-spline weights**
(`uniform_bspline_weights` in `numerics/_spline.py` — the cubic weights are the
familiar $(1-t)^3/6$, $(3t^3-6t^2+4)/6$, $\dots$, the same evaluator validated
for N4 bias-field correction). Because the weights are written out in closed
form rather than recursed, the construction is cuSOLVER-free and differentiable,
like the rest of the suite.

@fig-gam-basis shows the set of bumps. Note how each is a localised hump and how
they overlap their neighbours — that overlap is what lets the weighted sum be
*smooth* rather than a staircase.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gam-basis
#| fig-cap: "A uniform cubic B-spline basis: ten overlapping local humps over [0,1]. Each B_k is nonzero over only a few knot-spans (locality); the dashed line shows the partition of unity (the basis values sum to 1 everywhere)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def bspline_design(x, n_basis, degree=3, lo=0.0, hi=1.0):
    # Uniform cubic B-spline design, mirroring nitrix.numerics._spline.
    n_spans = n_basis - degree
    s = (x - lo) / (hi - lo) * n_spans
    span = np.clip(np.floor(s).astype(int), 0, n_spans - 1)
    t = np.clip(s - span, 0.0, 1.0)
    t2, t3 = t**2, t**3
    w = np.stack([                       # the four cubic B-spline weights
        (1 - t)**3 / 6.0,
        (3*t3 - 6*t2 + 4) / 6.0,
        (-3*t3 + 3*t2 + 3*t + 1) / 6.0,
        t3 / 6.0,
    ], axis=-1)
    B = np.zeros((x.size, n_basis))
    for i in range(x.size):
        for j in range(degree + 1):
            B[i, span[i] + j] += w[i, j]
    return B

x = np.linspace(0, 1, 400)
K = 10
B = bspline_design(x, K)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.2))
for k in range(K):
    ax.plot(x, B[:, k], lw=2)
ax.plot(x, B.sum(axis=1), "k--", lw=1, label="partition of unity (rows sum to 1)")
ax.set(title=f"{K} uniform cubic B-spline basis functions",
       xlabel="x", ylabel="$B_k(x)$")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

### The other bases `nitrix` ships

A B-spline is the default 1-D smoother, but `basis.py` exposes a family of
constructors, each a `SplineBasis` carrying a `(design, penalty)` pair plus the
parameters to re-evaluate the smooth on a fresh grid (for plotting partial
effects). All of them are real and grounded in source:

- **Thin-plate regression spline** (`thinplate_regression_basis`, `mgcv`'s
  `bs='tp'`): isotropic and knot-free. It starts from the full thin-plate basis
  (one radial function $|x-z_j|^3$ per knot) and *truncates* it by an
  eigendecomposition of the radial penalty, keeping the leading wiggly
  components plus a low-order polynomial null space (Wood 2003). The truncating
  `eigh` is a one-off host computation, so it dodges the broken GPU's `syevd`.
- **Cyclic cubic P-spline** (`cyclic_cubic_basis`, `bs='cp'`): for periodic
  covariates (cortical angle, phase, time-of-day) — the basis wraps at the
  period so $f$ and its derivatives match at the ends, with a *circular*
  difference penalty.
- **Cubic regression spline** (`cr_basis`, `bs='cr'`): a natural cubic spline
  parameterised by its values at the knots, with the *exact* integrated-curvature
  penalty $\int f''(x)^2\,dx$ assembled in `_cr_construct`.
- **Gaussian-process / kriging smooth** (`gp_basis`, `bs='gp'`): a low-rank
  Matérn-3/2 kriging basis — the bridge to the [HSGP](hsgp.qmd) chapter, where
  the penalty literally *is* an inverse covariance.
- **Markov-random-field smooth** (`mrf_smooth`, `bs='mrf'`): smoothing over a
  graph or parcellation, with the combinatorial graph Laplacian as penalty so
  neighbouring regions are shrunk together.
- **Tensor-product interaction** (`tensor_product_basis`): a 2-D (or higher)
  smooth $f(x_1, x_2)$ built from marginal bases by the row-wise Kronecker
  product of their designs, with one smoothing parameter *per margin*. We return
  to it in [tensor-product smooths](#sec-tensor).

Every constructor takes `center=True` by default — the
[identifiability fix](#sec-identifiability) — and conforms to the `SmoothBasis`
protocol, so `gam_fit` dispatches through
`design` / `dim` / `penalty_blocks()` / `eval_design()` and a new basis type
needs no edit to `gam.py`.

## The catch, and the cure: a roughness penalty {#sec-penalty}

A rich basis overfits — with $K$ free humps it will happily chase noise into
wiggles. The naive fix is to use fewer humps, but that is a blunt instrument: it
imposes a single global stiffness and throws away resolution everywhere to tame
overfitting somewhere. The better fix keeps the rich basis and instead
**penalises wiggliness directly.**

What is "wiggliness"? For a curve built from B-splines, where each coefficient
$\gamma_k$ controls the height of one local hump, a *smooth* curve is one whose
neighbouring coefficients change gradually; a *wiggly* curve is one whose
adjacent coefficients jump up and down. So a natural measure of roughness is the
size of the **second differences** of the coefficient sequence — the discrete
analogue of curvature. Define the second-difference operator $D$ by

$$
(D\gamma)_k = \gamma_{k} - 2\gamma_{k+1} + \gamma_{k+2},
$$ {#eq-diffop}

a $(K-2)\times K$ matrix that is zero except for the stencil $[1,-2,1]$ marching
down its rows. Then $\lVert D\gamma\rVert^2 = \sum_k (D\gamma)_k^2$ is large
exactly when the curve bends sharply and small when it is nearly linear. Writing
$\lVert D\gamma\rVert^2 = \gamma^{\top}(D^{\top}D)\gamma$ gives us the **penalty
matrix**

$$
S = D^{\top}D ,
$$ {#eq-penmat}

a banded, symmetric, positive-*semi*definite matrix. This is exactly what
`difference_penalty_1d(n, order=2)` returns in `numerics/_spline.py`: it forms
$D = $ `np.diff(np.eye(n), n=2, axis=0)` and returns $D^{\top}D$. (The default
`penalty_order=2` penalises curvature; order 1 would penalise slope, order 3
jerk.) This is the **P-spline** of Eilers & Marx — a B-spline basis with a
discrete difference penalty — and it is the smooth `nitrix` ships as default.

Now the fit is a single penalised least-squares (here written for the smooth
coefficients $\gamma$; in the assembled model $z$ and weights $W$ come from the
GLM's IRLS step):

$$
\min_{\gamma}\ \underbrace{\lVert z - B\gamma\rVert_W^2}_{\text{fit}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\lambda\,\gamma^{\top}S\gamma}_{\text{roughness}} ,
$$ {#eq-penobj}

where $\lambda \ge 0$ tunes the trade-off: $\lambda\to 0$ interpolates the noise;
$\lambda\to\infty$ drives $D\gamma\to 0$, i.e. forces a straight line.
Differentiating @eq-penobj and setting the gradient to zero gives the GLM's
weighted normal equations with **one added term** (the assembled design $X$ now
holds the intercept, any parametric columns, and the smooth's $B$; $\beta$ stacks
all their coefficients):

$$
(X^{\top}W X + \lambda S)\,\beta = X^{\top}W z .
$$ {#eq-pennormal}

That is the *only* change from the [GLM's IRLS solve](glm.qmd#sec-irls): a
penalty $\lambda S$ added inside the same SPD solve. `nitrix` literally calls the
same penalised-IRLS core (`fit_penalised_irls`, via `_penalised_irls` in
`gam.py`); a plain GLM passes $S=0$, the GAM passes the smooth's penalty.

### The penalty's null space — why $S$ is rank-deficient

There is a subtlety worth dwelling on, because it explains an otherwise puzzling
fact: $S=D^{\top}D$ does *not* penalise everything. A second difference is zero
for any coefficient sequence that is **constant or linear** in $k$ — a flat or
straight line has no curvature to punish. Those sequences live in the **null
space** of $D$ (and hence of $S$), so $S$ has rank $K-2$, not $K$. The penalty
leaves the constant and linear parts of the fit *free*; it only shrinks the
genuinely wiggly components. That is exactly the behaviour we want — and it is
the reason the fit collapses to a line, not to zero, as $\lambda\to\infty$.

@fig-gam-penalty shows $D$ and $S=D^{\top}D$ as images: the banded stencil in
$D$, and the resulting five-banded, rank-deficient $S$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gam-penalty
#| fig-cap: "The P-spline machinery. Left: the second-difference operator D, the stencil [1,-2,1] marching down its rows. Right: the penalty S = DᵀD — symmetric, five-banded, and rank-deficient (it does not penalise the constant or linear part: its 2-D null space)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def difference_penalty(n, order):
    D = np.diff(np.eye(n), n=order, axis=0)   # the order-th difference operator
    return D, D.T @ D

K = 12
D, S = difference_penalty(K, 2)               # second-difference (curvature) penalty

fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 3.4))
im0 = axs[0].imshow(D, cmap="RdBu", vmin=-2, vmax=2)
axs[0].set(title=r"$D$: second-difference operator  $(K-2)\times K$",
           xlabel="coefficient", ylabel="row")
fig.colorbar(im0, ax=axs[0], fraction=0.046)

im1 = axs[1].imshow(S, cmap="RdBu", vmin=-S.max(), vmax=S.max())
rank = np.linalg.matrix_rank(S)
axs[1].set(title=rf"$S=D^\top D$  (banded, rank {rank} of {K})",
           xlabel="coefficient", ylabel="coefficient")
fig.colorbar(im1, ax=axs[1], fraction=0.046)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Identifiability: the smooth that fights the intercept {#sec-identifiability}

A smooth has a hidden redundancy with the model intercept. You can add a constant
$c$ to every value of $f(x)$ and subtract $c$ from the intercept and the fitted
values do not move at all. The model cannot tell the two apart — the parameters
are **unidentifiable**, the relevant block of $X^{\top}WX$ is singular, and the
solve is ambiguous.

The standard cure is a **sum-to-zero constraint**: require the smooth to average
to zero over the data, $\sum_i f(x_i) = 0$, i.e. $(\mathbf{1}^{\top}B)\gamma = 0$.
This pins down the constant once and for all — the intercept owns the overall
level, the smooth owns only the *shape*. The question is how to *impose* the
single linear constraint $c^{\top}\gamma = 0$ (with $c = B^{\top}\mathbf{1}$, the
column sums of $B$) without making the solver carry it around.

The trick is to **reparameterise** rather than constrain. We seek a new,
unconstrained coordinate $\alpha$ with one fewer dimension, related by
$\gamma = Z\alpha$, such that *every* $\alpha$ automatically satisfies the
constraint — that is, $Z$'s columns must be an orthonormal basis of the null
space of $c^{\top}$ (so $c^{\top}Z = 0$). Then we fit $\alpha$ freely, with the
reparameterised design $BZ$ and penalty $Z^{\top}SZ$, and the constraint can
never be violated. The intuition: *rotate to a coordinate system in which the
constraint direction has been deleted.*

Textbooks build $Z$ with a QR factorisation, but `nitrix` cannot call cuSOLVER's
`qr` on the broken GPU. Since there is only **one** constraint to absorb, a full
QR is overkill: a single **Householder reflection** does it. `_householder_null`
in `basis.py` constructs the reflection $H$ that maps $c$ onto the first
coordinate axis ($Hc \propto e_1$), and takes $Z = H[:, 1:]$ — the reflection's
last $K-1$ columns. Because $H$ is orthogonal and $Hc$ points along $e_1$, those
columns are orthonormal and orthogonal to $c$, exactly the null-space basis we
need. It is pure elementwise algebra (an outer product and a subtraction), so it
is cuSOLVER-free and differentiable. Every `*_basis` constructor applies it when
`center=True` (the default), replacing `design = design @ constraint` and
`penalty = constraint.T @ penalty @ constraint`; the returned basis then has
$K-1$ columns, and the constraint is stored so partial effects re-evaluate in the
same reparameterised coordinates.

## Two loops, both already built

Fitting a GAM is two nested loops, and you have seen both:

- **Inner** (fixed $\lambda$): solve @eq-pennormal by penalised IRLS — the
  [GLM](glm.qmd#sec-irls) fit with the penalty added. For the Gaussian identity
  link this converges in one step, and `gam.py` exploits that with a
  cross-product fast path (`_gam_fit_one_gaussian_xprod`) that runs the whole
  per-voxel loop in $p$-space, touching the $N$-dimensional data only once.
- **Outer** (choose $\lambda$): the [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update)
  update, which raises the marginal likelihood and keeps $\lambda$ positive.

Nothing here is new machinery; a GAM is the GLM solver wrapped in the smoothing-
parameter selector. (See `stats/gam.py`, `stats/basis.py`.)

## The hinge: a penalty *is* a variance {#sec-hinge}

Why does Fellner–Schall — a *variance-component* estimator from the
[LME](lme.qmd) chapter — choose a *smoothing* parameter? Because they are the same
thing. The roughness penalty $\lambda\gamma^{\top}S\gamma$ is exactly the
negative log-density of a Gaussian prior $\gamma \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,\sigma_b^2 S^{-})$
(with $S^{-}$ a generalised inverse, since $S$ is rank-deficient — the
unpenalised null space [described above](#sec-penalty) is the *flat* prior on the
constant/linear part),
and the smoothing parameter is a ratio of variances:

$$
\lambda = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\sigma_b^2}.
$$ {#eq-equiv}

Read left to right it is a *smoothing parameter*; read right to left it is a
*ratio of variances*. So a smooth term **is a random effect**, a GAM **is an
LME**, and "how smooth?" **is** "how big is the random-effect variance?". This is
the equivalence derived in full on the
[foundations page](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence) and stated as a theorem on
the [Fellner–Schall page](fellner-schall.qmd#equivalence); it is the single
reason the whole suite shares one engine instead of being a pile of separate
fitters. (We keep it crisp here — the derivation lives in foundations.)

The prior reading also says what kind of assumption a smooth makes. The penalty
declares an **inductive bias** — *the truth is smooth* — exactly as the prior
$\gamma\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_b^2 S^{-})$ concentrates probability on gently
curving functions; it is the statistical cousin of the symmetry assumptions the
[geometry unit](../geometry/index.qmd) builds into its operators. The bias is
declared in the *form* of $S$ (second differences ⇒ penalise curvature) and
*calibrated* in its scale $\lambda$ by Fellner–Schall maximising the marginal
likelihood — empirical Bayes, the data setting the strength of its own prior.

Concretely, this is why `re_smooth` in `basis.py` works: a random-effect block
is nothing but a smooth whose penalty is the **identity** ($S=I$, a ridge), and the
smoothing parameter the same Fellner–Schall loop selects for it *is* the
random-effect precision $1/\sigma_b^2$. No new solver — a random effect is one
more penalty block.

## How flexible was the fit? Effective degrees of freedom {#sec-edf}

A penalised smooth has $K$ coefficients but is *not* as flexible as $K$ free
parameters — the penalty ties them together, so it "spends" fewer than $K$. How
many does it really spend? Look at the **smoother (hat) matrix**, the linear map
from the working response to the fitted values:

$$
A = X(X^{\top}W X + \lambda S)^{-1}X^{\top}W .
$$ {#eq-hat}

For an ordinary unpenalised linear model ($S=0$, or $\lambda=0$) this is the
familiar projection onto the column space of $X$, and $\operatorname{tr}(A)$
equals $p$, the number of parameters — the textbook "degrees of freedom". The
beautiful fact is that this *still* reads as a parameter count when a penalty is
present: the **effective degrees of freedom**

$$
\text{EDF} = \operatorname{tr}(A)
= \operatorname{tr}\!\big[(X^{\top}W X + \lambda S)^{-1} X^{\top}W X\big] .
$$ {#eq-edf}

(The two forms are equal by the cyclic property of the trace.) Intuitively each
diagonal entry of $A$ measures how much a fitted value depends on its *own* data
point: a free parameter contributes $\approx 1$, a heavily shrunk one contributes
$\approx 0$, and the trace adds these "fractions of a parameter" up. As $\lambda$
grows the penalty borrows strength across coefficients and the count falls; as
$\lambda\to 0$ it rises back toward $K$. So EDF interpolates between a straight
line (EDF $\to 2$, the dimension of the unpenalised null space — a constant and a
slope) and an interpolant (EDF $\to K$, the basis dimension). It is the honest
"how many parameters did this smooth really use".

`gam.py` computes exactly this. After the fit it forms the influence matrix
`influence = V @ xtwx` with $V = (X^{\top}WX + \lambda S)^{-1}$ the Bayesian
covariance (the same matrix used for confidence bands and the
`smooth_significance` tests), takes its diagonal, and sums the entries belonging
to each smooth's column block — the per-smooth `edf`, with the total in
`edf_total`. @fig-gam-edf traces EDF as a function of $\lambda$: a smooth,
monotone curve from the basis dimension down to 2, the dial that "how smooth?"
turns.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gam-edf
#| fig-cap: "Effective degrees of freedom EDF = tr(A) as a function of the smoothing parameter λ, for a K=20 P-spline. Flexibility trades smoothly against penalty: EDF runs from K (unpenalised, right) down to 2 (penalised to a straight line, left). Fellner–Schall picks one point on this curve."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def bspline_design(x, n_basis, degree=3, lo=0.0, hi=1.0):
    n_spans = n_basis - degree
    s = (x - lo) / (hi - lo) * n_spans
    span = np.clip(np.floor(s).astype(int), 0, n_spans - 1)
    t = np.clip(s - span, 0.0, 1.0)
    t2, t3 = t**2, t**3
    w = np.stack([(1 - t)**3 / 6, (3*t3 - 6*t2 + 4)/6,
                  (-3*t3 + 3*t2 + 3*t + 1)/6, t3/6], axis=-1)
    B = np.zeros((x.size, n_basis))
    for i in range(x.size):
        for j in range(degree + 1):
            B[i, span[i] + j] += w[i, j]
    return B

n, K = 200, 20
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
B = bspline_design(x, K)
D = np.diff(np.eye(K), n=2, axis=0); S = D.T @ D
BtB = B.T @ B

# EDF(lambda) = tr[(BᵀB + lambda S)⁻¹ BᵀB], the trace of the smoother matrix.
lams = np.logspace(-6, 6, 200)
edf = np.array([np.trace(np.linalg.solve(BtB + lam*S, BtB)) for lam in lams])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
ax.semilogx(lams, edf, lw=2.5, color="C0")
ax.axhline(K, ls=":", color="0.5"); ax.axhline(2, ls=":", color="0.5")
ax.text(lams[0], K - 0.6, f"unpenalised: EDF → K = {K}", fontsize=8, va="top")
ax.text(lams[-1], 2.4, "fully penalised: EDF → 2 (a line)",
        fontsize=8, ha="right")
ax.set(title="effective degrees of freedom vs smoothing parameter",
       xlabel=r"$\lambda$ (smoothing parameter)", ylabel="EDF = tr(A)")
ax.invert_xaxis()
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

EDF is not only diagnostic: it feeds the dispersion estimate (residual variance
divides by $N - \text{EDF}$, not $N - p$) and the approximate significance tests
(`smooth_significance` implements Wood's 2013 `summary.gam` test, with the
smooth's rounded EDF as its reference degrees of freedom).

## Beyond one dimension: tensor-product smooths {#sec-tensor}

What if the effect depends on *two* covariates jointly — say a thickness surface
over (age, a regional coordinate) that is not separable into a sum of 1-D
effects? Build a marginal basis for each covariate and take their
**tensor product**: the 2-D basis function is the product $B^{(1)}_a(x_1)\,
B^{(2)}_b(x_2)$ of one hump from each margin, so the surface is $f(x_1,x_2) =
\sum_{a,b} \gamma_{ab}\,B^{(1)}_a(x_1)B^{(2)}_b(x_2)$. `tensor_product_basis` in
`basis.py` forms this by the **row-wise Kronecker product** of the marginal
designs (`_row_kron`), giving a $(n, \prod_j k_j)$ design.

The key design choice is the penalty. A tensor smooth should be allowed to be
*rougher in one direction than the other* (anisotropic), so it carries **one
penalty per margin**, $S_j = I \otimes \cdots \otimes S^{(j)} \otimes \cdots
\otimes I$ — penalty $j$ acts on margin $j$'s wiggliness and is the identity on
the others. The Fellner–Schall loop already handles more than one penalty per
smooth (its `fs` step is `vmap`ped over penalty blocks), so it selects a separate
$\lambda_j$ per margin. The marginal penalties commute, so they are
simultaneously diagonalisable; `TensorBasis` precomputes the joint-eigenbasis
eigenvalues (`pen_eig`) so the smoothing-parameter trace
$\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{+}S_k)$ in `_trace_slinv_sk` is an elementwise sum —
cuSOLVER-free, no per-iteration pseudo-inverse — while the fit itself stays in the
original tensor basis.

## Too little, too much, just right

Three fits of one smooth at three penalty strengths — pure NumPy, build-time. The
middle is roughly what Fellner–Schall finds.

```{python}
#| label: fig-smoothing
#| fig-cap: "The smoothing trade-off. λ too small chases noise; λ too large flattens the signal; a balanced λ (what Fellner–Schall selects) recovers the shape."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 200
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
f_true = np.sin(2*np.pi*x) + 0.3*x
y = f_true + rng.normal(0, 0.35, n)

K = 30                                            # Gaussian-bump basis
knots = np.linspace(0, 1, K); h = 1/(K-1)
B = np.exp(-0.5*((x[:, None]-knots[None, :])/(1.2*h))**2)
D = np.diff(np.eye(K), n=2, axis=0); S = D.T @ D  # 2nd-difference roughness
BtB, Bty = B.T @ B, B.T @ y

def fit(lam):                                     # one penalised solve, eq-pennormal
    return B @ np.linalg.solve(BtB + lam*S, Bty)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=8, alpha=0.25, color="0.5", label="data")
ax.plot(x, f_true, "k--", lw=1.5, label="truth")
for lam, name in [(1e-4, "λ too small (wiggly)"), (1e3, "λ too large (stiff)"), (1.0, "λ balanced")]:
    ax.plot(x, fit(lam), lw=2, label=name)
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=2); ax.set(title="one smooth, three penalty strengths")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Where this goes

- Keep a **non-Gaussian family** and it is still a GAM — the inner loop already
  handles it (counts, binary).
- Add explicit **random-effect blocks** alongside the smooths and you get a
  [GAMM](gamm.qmd) — and since a smooth already *is* a random effect
  ([the hinge](#sec-hinge)), that is barely a new model at all.
- Swap the difference penalty for a **kernel** penalty $S = K^{-1}$ and the smooth
  becomes a [Gaussian process](hsgp.qmd).

## Further reading

- Wood, S. N. (2017). *Generalized Additive Models: An Introduction with R*, 2nd
  ed. Chapman & Hall/CRC. — The standard reference; basis construction, the
  penalty/variance equivalence, EDF, and the `mgcv` smoothers all live here.
- Eilers, P. H. C. & Marx, B. D. (1996). *Flexible smoothing with B-splines and
  penalties.* Statistical Science 11(2), 89–121. — The original P-spline paper
  (B-spline basis + discrete difference penalty), the default smooth `nitrix`
  ships.
- Wood, S. N. (2011). *Fast stable restricted maximum likelihood and marginal
  likelihood estimation of semiparametric generalized linear models.* Journal of
  the Royal Statistical Society, Series B 73(1), 3–36. — REML smoothing-parameter
  selection, the theory behind the outer loop.
- Wood, S. N. (2003). *Thin plate regression splines.* Journal of the Royal
  Statistical Society, Series B 65(1), 95–114. — The eigen-truncation behind
  `thinplate_regression_basis`.
- Wood, S. N. & Fasiolo, M. (2017). *A generalized Fellner–Schall method for
  smoothing parameter optimization with application to Tweedie location, scale
  and shape models.* Biometrics 73(4), 1071–1081. — The update `gam.py` uses.
- Hastie, T. & Tibshirani, R. (1990). *Generalized Additive Models.* Chapman &
  Hall. — The original GAM monograph (backfitting and the additive idea).

## See also

- Code: [`nitrix/stats/gam.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gam.py),
  [`nitrix/stats/basis.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/basis.py).
- The selector: [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd). The equivalence's
  foundation: [the grand equivalence](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence) and
  [linear mixed-effects models](lme.qmd). The inner solve:
  [the GLM's IRLS](glm.qmd#sec-irls).
- Previous rung: [GLM](glm.qmd). Next rung: [GLMM](glmm.qmd), then
  [GAMM](gamm.qmd) and [HSGP](hsgp.qmd).
